series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/8] mm: memcontrol: propagate NMI slab stats to memcg vmstats
series_items.patch_summary: The patch series aims to propagate NMI slab stats to memcg vmstats, fixing an inconsistency in the handling of per-node and memcg-level statistics. The first patch addresses this issue by updating the `flush_nmi_stats` function to also update memcg-level vmstats.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Requested clarification in the commit message and suggested rephrasing a sentence for better clarity. Also, pointed out that three code lines should be kept together.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/8] mm: percpu: charge obj_exts allocation with __GFP_ACCOUNT
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the per-cpu memory allocation system to charge the metadata and payload allocations separately, allowing for more accurate accounting of memory usage. The patch does this by passing the __GFP_ACCOUNT flag to the obj_exts allocation and removing its accounting in pcpu_memcg_pre_alloc_hook(). This is a preparatory step for upcoming per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting. The changes affect two files: mm/percpu-internal.h and mm/percpu.c, with 11 insertions and 20 deletions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/8] mm: percpu: Split memcg charging and kmem accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits memory group (memcg) charging and kernel memory (kmem) accounting into two separate steps to improve per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting. The first step, obj_cgroup_precharge(), reserves pages for memcg limit enforcement without updating kmem stats. The second step, obj_cgroup_account_kmem(), accounts kmem after the allocation succeeds and places any remaining sub-page remainder into the object's stock. This decoupling allows for temporary overcharging of memcg but ensures that kmem accounting is accurate in the long run.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/8] mm: memcontrol: track MEMCG_KMEM per NUMA node
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the MEMCG_KMEM feature and instead introduces per-node obj_cgroup objects to track memory usage. It does not convert existing kmem users to proper per-memcg-per-node accounting, which is handled in subsequent patches. The changes include updates to memcontrol.h, mmzone.h, and memcontrol.c files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/8] mm: memcontrol: per-node kmem accounting for page charges
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the __memcg_kmem_charge_page function to use per-node object groups for correct NUMA attribution of kernel memory. The change involves introducing a new variable nid_objcg, which is used to get the object group associated with the node where the page resides. This ensures that kernel memory charges are attributed correctly based on the node where the page is allocated.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/8] mm: slab: per-node kmem accounting for slab
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the slab hook to use per-node object cgroups for accurate NUMA attribution of kernel memory usage (NR_KMEM). It achieves this by introducing a new nid_objcg variable, which retrieves the per-node object cgroup based on the node ID of the slab. The patch modifies several functions within the memcontrol.c file to utilize the nid_objcg instead of the global objcg. This change ensures that kernel memory usage is attributed correctly to each NUMA node, providing a more accurate picture of system resource utilization.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 7/8] mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting using local credit
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces per-node kmem accounting using a local credit system to handle precharged pages in the percpu memory allocation code. The previous approach used obj_stock, which is no longer suitable due to decoupling of memcg charging and kmem accounting. The new approach ensures that enough charged pages are available for allocation by maintaining a node-specific credit that gets refilled into the stock when necessary.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 8/8] mm: zswap: per-node kmem accounting for zswap/zsmalloc
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the zswap and zsmalloc modules to use per-node object cgroup for kernel memory accounting, ensuring that compressed page charges are attributed to the correct NUMA node. However, it does not correctly handle entries that span multiple pages, which will be addressed in a separate series by Joshua Hahn. The patch introduces a new function `zs_handle_to_nid` to determine the NUMA node associated with a given zsmalloc handle and updates the `zswap_store_page` function to use per-node object cgroup for charging. This change is necessary to accurately account for memory usage on systems with multiple NUMA nodes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to introduce per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting, allowing for accurate tracking of kernel memory usage on a per-node basis. The patches modify various parts of the Linux kernel to implement this new accounting mechanism, including changes to memcontrol, slab, and zswap.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] btrfs: always drop root->inodes lock before cond_resched()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] btrfs: always drop root->inodes lock before cond_resched()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch addresses a lock contention issue in the btrfs extent map shrinker, which can cause memory pressure due to kswapd getting stuck in inode slab shrinking. The fix involves replacing `cond_resched_lock()` with an unconditional unlock/lock around `cond_resched()` to decouple lock sharing from CPU sharing.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Identified the issue as a lock contention problem and confirmed that the proposed fix resolves it. Provided a formal Reviewed-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 7:24 PM Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io> wrote:
>
> find_first_inode() and find_first_inode_to_shrink() lock root->inodes,
> then loop over them, occasionally skipping some inodes. When they skip
> an inode, they attempt to share the cpu/lock with cond_resched_lock().
>
> However, that has a subtle problem associated with it.
> cond_resched_lock() only drops the lock if it needs to actually call
> schedule(). With CONFIG_PREEMPT_NONE, this means the full timeslice as
> detected at ticks. With 8+ cpus and default tunables, this is 2.8ms. So
> regardless of HZ, we will run for at least 2.8ms in this loop without
> dropping the lock, assuming it finds no suitable inodes. If HZ is
> small enough, it might be even worse as the tick granularity becomes
> bigger than the timeslice.
>
> The knock-on effect of this is that callers to
> btrfs_del_inode_from_root() like kswapd trying to shrink the inode slab
> or userspace threads calling evict() will spin on xa_lock(&root->inodes)
> for 2.8ms, so the extent map shrinker dominates the lock even though
> ostensibly it is intending to share it. This produces memory pressure as
> there is only one kswapd and it runs sequentially so it can get stuck in
> the inode slab shrinking.
>
> To fix it, simply replace cond_resched_lock() with an open coded variant
> which unconditionally does unlock/lock around cond_resched. Sharing the
> lock is decoupled from sharing the cpu, and all the users of the lock
> now share it fairly.
>
> I was able to reproduce this on test systems by producing a lot of empty
> files (to make a big root->inodes xarray), then producing memory
> pressure by reading large files larger than ram, triggering kswapd and
> the extent_map shrinker. The lock contention is visible with perf or
> lockstat. This patch also relieved a user-apparent bottleneck on a
> production system from the original report.
>
> Tested-by: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

Reviewed-by: Filipe Manana <fdmanana@suse.com>

Looks good, thanks.

> ---
> Changelog:
> v2:
> - apply the pattern to btrfs_find_first_inode() as well.
>
> ---
>  fs/btrfs/extent_map.c | 4 +++-
>  fs/btrfs/inode.c      | 4 +++-
>  2 files changed, 6 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c b/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c
> index 514d7b59cbe5..fce9c5cc0122 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/extent_map.c
> @@ -1246,7 +1246,9 @@ static struct btrfs_inode *find_first_inode_to_shrink(struct btrfs_root *root,
>                 write_unlock(&tree->lock);
>  next:
>                 from = btrfs_ino(inode) + 1;
> -               cond_resched_lock(&root->inodes.xa_lock);
> +               xa_unlock(&root->inodes);
> +               cond_resched();
> +               xa_lock(&root->inodes);
>         }
>         xa_unlock(&root->inodes);
>
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/inode.c b/fs/btrfs/inode.c
> index 0e72408c8612..201010ced09d 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/inode.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/inode.c
> @@ -10540,7 +10540,9 @@ struct btrfs_inode *btrfs_find_first_inode(struct btrfs_root *root, u64 min_ino)
>                         break;
>
>                 from = btrfs_ino(inode) + 1;
> -               cond_resched_lock(&root->inodes.xa_lock);
> +               xa_unlock(&root->inodes);
> +               cond_resched();
> +               xa_lock(&root->inodes);
>         }
>         xa_unlock(&root->inodes);
>
> --
> 2.54.0
>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: always drop root->inodes lock in find_first_inode_to_shrink()

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: always drop root->inodes lock in find_first_inode_to_shrink()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a lock contention issue in the Btrfs extent map shrinker by always dropping the root->inodes lock in the find_first_inode_to_shrink() function. The original code would sometimes spin on the lock for up to 2.8ms, causing memory pressure and performance issues. The patch replaces the cond_resched_lock() call with an unconditional unlock/lock around cond_resched(), ensuring that the lock is shared fairly among users. This fix was tested and verified by the submitter and another developer.

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Filipe Manana questioned why the patch's change to use an unconditional unlock/lock around cond_resched is specific to find_first_inode_to_shrink(), pointing out that cond_resched_lock() is used in other places, including btrfs_find_first_inode(), which uses the same lock.
review_comments.raw_body:
We call cond_resched_lock() in other places, including
btrfs_find_first_inode(), which uses the same lock.
Why do that only here?

Thanks.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a concern about lock contention in find_first_inode_to_shrink(), agreed to restructure the function in v2, and asked for feedback on whether to also change other uses of cond_resched_lock()
review_comments.raw_body:
Good point. I was just narrowly following the profile and what I managed
to reproduce directly.

I did consider whether it made sense to change cond_resched_lock()
itself but decided to do just this to try out the idea to start.

I will definitely change btrfs_find_first_inode() in v2. Would you like
me to also get rid of the other uses of cond_resched_lock() from btrfs,
assuming it makes similar sense at each callsite?

review_comments.author: Filipe Manana
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Filipe Manana suggested that this patch should only address the lock root->inodes.xa_lock and not introduce additional changes, recommending a more targeted approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think this patch should only address the lock root->inodes.xa_lock
(all the places that call cond_resched_lock() for it).
And then other patches for other locks.

Thanks.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH resend v6 03/30] mm: thread user_addr through page allocator for cache-friendly zeroing
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch aims to thread user_addr through the page allocator for cache-friendly zeroing, but a reviewer raised concerns about sentinel correctness and potential issues with -1 as a valid address.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about sentinel correctness and potential issues with -1 as a valid address. Asked for clarification on whether this is correct on all architectures.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 05:59:23PM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 12:52:10PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> > On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 11:55:40AM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> > > On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 11:37:37AM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> > > > On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 05:01:55AM -0400, Michael S. Tsirkin wrote:
> > > > 
> > > > > +/*
> > > > > + * Sentinel for user_addr: indicates a non-user allocation.
> > > > > + * Cannot use 0 because address 0 is a valid userspace mapping.
> > > > > + */
> > > > > +#define USER_ADDR_NONE	((unsigned long)-1)
> > > > 
> > > > Ehm, hm.  Does -1 hold as a non-user address across all architectures?
> > > > 
> > > > What about in linear addressing / no VM mode?
> > > 
> > > this is used on a fault. I don't think there are any faults then?
> > > But maybe FAULT_ADDR_NONE would be clearer.
> > >
> > 
> > Meh, naming here is less relevant than the sentinel correctness.
> > 
> > My only concern is really whether -1 could end up being a valid address
> > in some horrid future timeline and this all going belly up.
> > 
> > Is why I asked about whether this is correct on all architectures.
> 
> I think the answer is yes: on all architectures Linux supports, the last
> page of the address space is never a valid user mapping. The kernel
> enforces this -- mmap will not create a mapping whose end wraps past -1.
> 
>   if (addr > TASK_SIZE - len)
>       return -ENOMEM;
> 
> 
> So the maximum vm_end = addr + len <= TASK_SIZE.
> On every architecture TASK_SIZE <= (unsigned long)-1, so vm_end can
> never wrap around to 0 and address -1 can never be within any
> VMA.
> 

That is worth documenting at least in the changelog if not the comment
with the define there's a clue if anything ever changes.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Michael Tsirkin
review_comments.summary: Reviewer questioned whether -1 is a valid user address across all architectures, noting that the kernel enforces this by not creating mappings whose end wraps past -1.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think the answer is yes: on all architectures Linux supports, the last
page of the address space is never a valid user mapping. The kernel
enforces this -- mmap will not create a mapping whose end wraps past -1.

  if (addr > TASK_SIZE - len)
      return -ENOMEM;


So the maximum vm_end = addr + len <= TASK_SIZE.
On every architecture TASK_SIZE <= (unsigned long)-1, so vm_end can
never wrap around to 0 and address -1 can never be within any
VMA.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 1/3] mm: preserve PG_dropbehind flag during folio split
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a bug where the PG_dropbehind flag is lost during folio splits, leading to silent data loss and delayed writeback. The fix involves adding PG_dropbehind to the explicit allowlist in __split_folio_to_order().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 2/3] mm: track DONTCACHE dirty pages per bdi_writeback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new counter, WB_DONTCACHE_DIRTY, to track the number of dirty pages that have been dirtied via RWF_DONTCACHE writes. The counter is incremented when a folio is dirtied and decremented when it is cleaned or cleared. This information will be used by the writeback flusher to determine how many pages to write back when expediting writeback for IOCB_DONTCACHE writes, without flushing the entire BDI's dirty pages. The patch also updates the inode_do_switch_wbs function to transfer the counter alongside WB_RECLAIMABLE when an inode switches cgroup writeback domains.

review_comments.author: Christian Brauner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Christian Brauner expressed strong disagreement (CONTENTIOUS) with AI-assisted-by tags in kernel patches, considering them 'useless noise' and requesting that they be removed.
review_comments.raw_body:
Picking up on something we discussed at LSFMM in one of the sessions as
an aside rant: I find these AI Assisted-by tags so useless tbh and just
pure noise in the git log _especially_ for a core developer like Jeff
that I really don't see the point of them and I'm always tempted to just
remove the tags when I apply. I have dropped them before because I found
them so pointless.

Crediting Jan here is the right thing to do and it provides actual value
and also just makes sure that a real person who spent time helping out
gets visibility in the git history. Why we should extend the same
courtesy to automated tooling is really beyond me. Somehow we've become
all convinced that these tools require a special status but have spent
months arguing about the usefulness of other tags.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author responded to feedback about inconsistent application of Assisted-by tags, arguing that if the rule is applied to some contributors, it should be applied to all, and questioning whether adding these tags has any value.
review_comments.raw_body:
To be clear, Christoph and Ritesh also contributed a lot of review and
suggestions.

I was mainly trying to follow this new verbiage in
Documentation/process/submitting-patches.rst:

------------------8<-------------------
Using Assisted-by:
------------------
If you used any sort of advanced coding tool in the creation of your patch,
you need to acknowledge that use by adding an Assisted-by tag.  Failure to
do so may impede the acceptance of your work.  Please see
Documentation/process/coding-assistants.rst for details regarding the
acknowledgment of coding assistants.
------------------8<-------------------

If we're demanding this from anyone, then we should demand it from
everyone. I don't think we want one set of rules for core contributors
and another set for other folks.

As to whether we should add them at all -- I don't know. I think it
really comes down to what we intend to do with this info. I'll play
devil's advocate for the moment:

The cost of adding these tags is low. It's just a few extra bits in the
repo. Maybe this could eventually have historical value?
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David expressed concern that the patch description does not provide enough information about how AI tooling was used, and suggested that it should be more detailed to avoid unchecked AI output.
sentiment_signals: lack of detail in patch description
review_comments.raw_body:
As we don't really know "how" AI tooling helped. it's pretty useless without a
more detailed description I'm afraid.

OTOH, we want people (in particular not trusted community members) to indicate
that it might all just be unchecked AI output.

Hm.

-- 
Cheers,

David

series_items.subject: [PATCH v7 3/3] mm: kick writeback flusher for IOCB_DONTCACHE with targeted dirty tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the writeback path in Linux by moving the submission of writeback work from the writer's context to a background flusher. This reduces lock contention and improves performance on systems using IOCB_DONTCACHE. The patch introduces a new WB_start_dontcache bit that allows for targeted dirty tracking, limiting the scope of expedited writeback while preserving I/O batching. The changes also include improvements to handling concurrent writes and cgroup writeback domain targeting.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series improves write performance by addressing issues related to RWF_DONTCACHE, a flag that prevents caching of dirty pages. The patches fix integer promotion bugs on 32-bit systems, ensure proper stat accounting during cgroup writeback domain migration and folio split operations, and prevent use-after-free (UAF) errors in filemap_dontcache_kick_writeback(). Additionally, the patches track DONTCACHE dirty pages per bdi_writeback, have the flusher write back a proportional number of pages after a DONTCACHE write, and include new benchmarks to test competing buffered and dontcache writers.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: try_break_deleg() and atomic_open()
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Discussion around handling of atomic_open() and delegation/lease management in the kernel.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Proposed checking for a lease before calling ->atomic_open(), allowing the lookup to be done separately if necessary. Suggested that an O_CREAT open should always break a directory lease, even if the name exists.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, 12 May 2026, Jeff Layton wrote:
> On Tue, 2026-05-12 at 08:21 +1000, NeilBrown wrote:
> > Hi Jeff,
> >  quick question (I hope).
> > Should atomic_open() call try_break_deleg() on the directory
> > when a create is pending?
> > 
> > This seems a bit iffy because the VFS doesn't necessarily know if a
> > create will happen before it calls ->atomic_open, so it cannot know
> > if it needs to break the deleg or not.
> 
> Agreed, so I'm thinking no to doing that in generic code.
> 
> > So maybe the individual ->atomic_open functions should do it?
> > 
> 
> I think that's probably what has to happen:
> 
> atomic_open() is there to handle the non-trivial open cases (mostly
> network and clustered filesystems). Those, in general, also require
> non-trivial delegation/lease handling. I think we sort of need to leave
> it to the underlying fs in those cases since the kernel doesn't have
> enough info to do it.

I had a look and could only find gfs2/nolocks and NFSv4 as filesystems
which support leases on directories and use ->atomic_open.

I wonder if gfs2/nolocks should not advertise ->atomic_open.  The
implication of nolocks is (I assume) that there is only the one active
client, and in that case no special handling is needed for exclusive
create.

NFSv4 uses delegations to provide leases.  So the ->atomic_open
handler does have work to do to cancel any lease while keeping the
delegation.
We would need to either allow ->atomic_open to return the deleg_inode
somehow, or have ->atomic_open drop the parent lock so that it can
safely wait.

Or we could just ignore the issue until I manage to land my changed to
push locking down into the filesystem, and then locking/waiting becomes
much easier.


> 
> > I'm looking at dentry_create() which calls atomic_open() is quite a
> > different way to how lookup_open() calls it.  I'd like to change
> > nfsd4 so it calls something a lot more like lookup_open() and in
> > looking at what I would need to change, delegated_inode stood out.
> > 
> 
> Understood. I wish that were a bit less klunky, but I don't see a great
> way to make it so.

We could check for a lease and if one is present then do the lookup
separately from ->atomic_open.  If that finds a match then no create is
needed.  If it doesn't then there is justification to break the lease
before calling ->atomic_open.

This means that when there is a lease on an NFS directory, other apps
have to do a LOOKUP for uncached names before sending a creating OPEN.
Maybe that is an acceptable cost.

Should an O_CREAT open *always* break a directory lease, even if the
name happens to exist?
I note that man/man2const/F_GETLEASE.2const in man-pages.git doesn't
mention directories.

Thanks,
NeilBrown


> 
> Cheers,
> -- 
> Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Agreed with Neil's proposal to check for a lease before calling ->atomic_open(). Suggested that the kernel may not have enough information to handle delegation/lease management and should leave it to the underlying filesystem.

review_comments.summary: Raised a question about the correctness of waking based on 'dentry', suggesting an alternative approach using __d_wake_in_lookup_waiters(target).

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged Jeff's catch and provided additional context, mentioning that a similar version of the patch is present in vfs-next/work.dcache.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, 12 May 2026, Jeff Layton wrote:
> On Thu, 2026-04-30 at 11:56 +1000, NeilBrown wrote:
> > From: NeilBrown <neil@brown.name>
> > 
> > d_alloc_parallel() currently requires a wait_queue_head to be passed in.
> > This must have a life time which extends until the lookup is completed.
> > 
> > This makes it awkward to use and particularly make it hard to use in
> > lookup_one_qstr_excl() which I hope to do in the future.
> > 
> > This patch changes d_alloc_parallel() to use wake_up_var_locked() to
> > wake up waiters, and wait_var_event_spinlock() to wait.  dentry->d_lock
> > is used for synchronisation as it is already held and the relevant
> > times.
> > 
> > In most cases there will be no waiters so the wake_up_var_locked() call
> > (which can walk an arbitrarily long list) would be a complete waste.  To
> > optimise this a new ->d_flags flag is added: DCACHE_LOOKUP_WAITER.  This
> > is set whenever any thread prepares to wait for the dentry, and if it
> > isn't set when DCACHE_PAR_LOOKUP is cleared, no wakeup is sent.
> > DCACHE_LOOKUP_WAITER is cleared after the wakeup is set.
> > 
> > __d_lookup_unhash() no longer returns a wq.  Callers check
> > DCACHE_LOOKUP_WAITER to check if a wakeup is needed, and
> > wake_up_var_locked() can find the wq.
> > 
> > __d_lookup_unhash() no longer needs to re-init ->d_lru.  That was
> > previously shared (in a union) with ->d_wait but ->d_wait is now gone
> > so it no longer corrupts ->d_lru.
> > 
> > Wakeup is move out of end_dir_add() as it is conceptually a separate action.
> > 
> > Co-developed-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
> > Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
> > Signed-off-by: NeilBrown <neil@brown.name>
> > ---
> >  Documentation/filesystems/porting.rst |  6 ++
> >  fs/afs/dir_silly.c                    |  4 +-
> >  fs/dcache.c                           | 83 +++++++++++++++------------
> >  fs/fuse/readdir.c                     |  3 +-
> >  fs/namei.c                            |  6 +-
> >  fs/nfs/dir.c                          |  6 +-
> >  fs/nfs/unlink.c                       |  3 +-
> >  fs/proc/base.c                        |  3 +-
> >  fs/proc/proc_sysctl.c                 |  3 +-
> >  fs/smb/client/readdir.c               |  3 +-
> >  include/linux/dcache.h                | 11 ++--
> >  include/linux/nfs_xdr.h               |  1 -
> >  12 files changed, 67 insertions(+), 65 deletions(-)
> > 
> > 
> 
> [...]
> 
> >  
> > @@ -2979,7 +2987,7 @@ static void __d_move(struct dentry *dentry, struct dentry *target,
> >  	if (unlikely(d_in_lookup(target))) {
> >  		dir = target->d_parent->d_inode;
> >  		n = start_dir_add(dir);
> > -		d_wait = __d_lookup_unhash(target);
> > +		__d_lookup_unhash(target);
> >  	}
> 
> In the old code, d_wait was associated with the target...
> 
> >  
> >  	write_seqcount_begin(&dentry->d_seq);
> > @@ -3018,9 +3026,10 @@ static void __d_move(struct dentry *dentry, struct dentry *target,
> >  	write_seqcount_end(&target->d_seq);
> >  	write_seqcount_end(&dentry->d_seq);
> >  
> > -	if (dir)
> > -		end_dir_add(dir, n, d_wait);
> > -
> > +	if (dir) {
> > +		end_dir_add(dir, n);
> > +		__d_wake_in_lookup_waiters(dentry);
> > +	}
> 
> ...but here you are waking based on "dentry". Should that be:
> 
> 		__d_wake_in_lookup_waiters(target)
> 
> ?

Good catch.  I needed a long test run and a hint from Al before I saw
that :-)
The version in vfs-next/work.dcache (c177d84a4476) has "target" as you
suggest.

Thanks,
NeilBrown

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 00/22] Virtual Swap Space

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 01/22] mm/swap: decouple swap cache from physical swap infrastructure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the Linux kernel's swap cache from its physical swap infrastructure, preparing for virtualization of swap space. The swap cache is currently managed at the physical swap layer, but will be moved to a virtual swap layer in future patches. To achieve this, the patch removes swap cache related helpers from the swap table and introduces a single global lock to synchronize swap cache accesses. This change also sets the stage for re-partitioning of the swap cache into virtual swap clusters.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Yosry Ahmed questioned the design decision to decouple swap cache from physical swap infrastructure, suggesting that reusing the swap table as the representation of virtual swap space could be a more efficient approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
I didn't look through the entire series, but let me ask the same
high-level question I asked before. Instead of moving things out of the
swap table, why not reuse the swap table as the representation of the
virtual swap space? Seems like most/all metadata is already moved there
in a nice concise format.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 02/22] swap: rearrange the swap header file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch rearranges the swap header file (include/linux/swap.h) to group related functions into categories such as lifecycle swap, swap cache, and physical swap allocator and device APIs. The extern keyword is removed from these functions. This change is a clean-up effort with no functional changes intended.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 03/22] mm: swap: add an abstract API for locking out swapoff
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces an abstract API to lock out swapoff operations in the Linux kernel. The current implementation of locking out swapoff relies on getting a reference to the backing swap device, which is not suitable for virtual swap designs where the swap entry may not have a backing device or its backend can change during its lifetime. The new API, provided by two inline functions `tryget_swap_entry` and `put_swap_entry`, allows for generic locking out of swapoff operations without requiring knowledge of the underlying swap device. This patch modifies several kernel files to use this new API, ensuring that swapoff is properly locked out in various scenarios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 04/22] zswap: add new helpers for zswap entry operations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds new helper functions to abstract away zswap entry operations, making it easier to re-implement these functions when swap is virtualized. The helpers include storing, loading, erasing, and checking if a zswap entry is empty. These functions are used in various places throughout the codebase, such as in the zswap_writeback_entry function. By introducing these abstractions, the patch aims to improve maintainability and flexibility of the zswap implementation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 05/22] mm/swap: add a new function to check if a swap entry is in swap cached.
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called is_swap_cached to check if a swap entry is in the swap cache. The function abstracts away the current implementation of checking the swap cached state, which will soon be managed at the virtual swap layer. It minimizes overhead by directly accessing the swap map using READ_ONCE. This change aims to simplify and future-proof the code for upcoming changes in swap cache management.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 06/22] mm: swap: add a separate type for physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new type, swp_slot_t, to represent physical swap slots on swap devices. It separates the logical view of swap entries from their physical backing state, allowing for future virtualization of swap. The mapping between the two types is an identity mapping, and the only intentional behavioral change is in swap_vma_readahead(), which now pins readahead entries via tryget_swap_entry()/put_swap_entry(). This patch is a mechanical refactor to prepare for dynamic allocation of virtual swap slots and association with backing stores.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 07/22] mm: create scaffolds for the new virtual swap implementation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds scaffolding code for virtual swap implementation, including a new source file mm/vswap.c and modifications to the Makefile and other files. The new code sets up a debugfs directory for virtual swap but does not introduce any behavioral changes yet. It also adds Johannes Weiner as a reviewer for virtual swap development.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 08/22] zswap: prepare zswap for swap virtualization
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prepares the zswap code for swap virtualization by removing its dependency on the range partition logic and replacing it with a simple unified zswap tree. The changes include updating the zswap.h header file to remove unused functions, modifying the swapfile.c file to remove calls to these functions, and updating the zswap.c file to use the new unified zswap tree. This patch is part of a larger series that aims to virtualize swap space in Linux.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 09/22] mm: swap: allocate a virtual swap slot for each swapped out page
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new virtual swap slot design for each swapped out page in the Linux kernel. It dynamically allocates a virtual slot and associates it with a physical swap slot on a swapfile or partition. The virtual swap slot is stored in page table entries and used to index into swap data structures, replacing the old physical swap slot. This change also updates the freeing ordering of swap slots and repurposes the swap table infrastructure as a reverse map for looking up virtual swap slots from their associated physical swap slots.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 10/22] swap: move swap cache to virtual swap descriptor
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the swap cache and workingset shadow for anonymous pages to the virtual swap descriptor, effectively range-partitioning the swap cache by virtual swap clusters of PMD size. This change eliminates lock contention in the swap cache. The patch modifies several kernel files, including huge_memory.c, migrate.c, shmem.c, swap.h, swap_state.c, vmscan.c, and vswap.c.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 11/22] zswap: move zswap entry management to the virtual swap descriptor
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves zswap entry management from a dedicated tree to the virtual swap descriptor, eliminating lock contention between zswap entries and reducing complexity in the codebase. The change involves modifying several files, including include/linux/zswap.h, mm/vswap.c, and mm/zswap.c, where new functions are added for storing, loading, and erasing zswap entries from the swap descriptor. The patch also includes a re-partitioning of the zswap pool by virtual swap cluster to improve performance. Overall, this change aims to simplify the code and reduce contention in the zswap system.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 12/22] swap: implement the swap_cgroup API using virtual swap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch implements the swap_cgroup API using virtual swap, which allows for dynamic allocation of memory overhead for swap cgroup information. This reduces the memory overhead in large but sparsely used swap spaces by only allocating memory when needed. The patch removes static arrays and instead stores swap entries' cgroup information in the swap descriptor. It also introduces a new function to record swap cgroup information, __swap_cgroup_record, which is called from the virtual swap cluster allocation code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 13/22] swap: manage swap entry lifecycle at the virtual swap layer
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the management of swap entry lifecycle to the virtual swap layer, adding two new fields to the swap descriptor: in_swapcache and swap count. It re-implements various swap entry lifecycle APIs in the virtual swap layer, simplifying logic by not implementing swap count continuation for now.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 14/22] mm: swap: decouple virtual swap slot from backing store
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples virtual swap slots from their backing storage, allowing them to be backed by physical swap slots, zero swap pages, compressed objects in the zswap pool, or in-memory pages. This change enables the use of zswap and zero swap page optimization without reserving a slot on a swapfile or using a swapfile at all, resulting in significant disk space savings for hosts with high memory usage. The patch also introduces new requirements for batched swap operations to ensure that entries have consistent backing states.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 15/22] zswap: do not start zswap shrinker if there is no physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents the zswap shrinker from starting if there are no physical swap slots available, which is a problem when swap is virtualized and pre-allocated slots on swapfile for each zswap entry are not used.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 16/22] swap: do not unnecessarily pin readahead swap entries
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes swap readahead by avoiding unnecessary pinning of swap entries in the same virtual swap cluster as the target entry. The patch introduces a new function `vswap_same_cluster` to check if two virtual swap entries belong to the same cluster, and uses this function to skip pinning entries that are already protected by the pinned target entry. This reduces overhead and improves performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 17/22] swapfile: remove zeromap bitmap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the zeromap bitmap from the swapfile code in the Linux kernel. The zeromap bitmap was used to track zero pages, but since zero swap entries are now treated as a separate backend in the virtual swap layer, this bitmap is no longer needed. Removing it saves memory overhead by reducing the number of bits required per swap page. This change does not affect the behavior of the system and only optimizes memory usage.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 18/22] memcg: swap: only charge physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory cgroup (memcg) swap handling to only charge physical swap slots, rather than virtual ones. This change is made possible by the removal of zswap and zero-filled swap page optimization from using physical swap space. The patch also optimizes IDR lookups on hot paths by converting memcg swap charge/uncharge/clear APIs to take a struct mem_cgroup pointer directly. Additionally, it reduces IDR lookups in several functions, including vswap_alloc_swap_slot(), vswap_free(), and memcg1_swapin(). Overall, this patch aims to improve performance and accuracy of memory cgroup swap handling.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 19/22] swap: simplify swapoff using virtual swap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies and optimizes the swapoff process by utilizing virtual swap slots stored at page table entries. It eliminates the need for a page table walk, instead iterating through allocated swap slots on the swapfile to fault in corresponding virtual swap slots. This approach is cleaner and slightly more performant, reducing kernel CPU time by about 13% compared to the original code. The patch modifies several files, including mm/swapfile.c, which has undergone significant changes to implement this new design.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 20/22] swapfile: replace the swap map with bitmaps
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces the swap map in Linux's swapfile code with two bitmaps, one for tracking allocated states and another for bad states. This change is made possible by moving the swap count state to the virtual swap layer, which reduces the number of bits required per swap entry from 6 to 1. The patch updates the swap_info_struct structure in include/linux/swap.h and modifies several functions in mm/swapfile.c to use the new bitmap-based approach.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 21/22] vswap: batch contiguous vswap free calls
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the vswap_free() function by batching contiguous free calls to reduce overhead on non-disk-swap backends, where releasing and reacquiring the cluster lock is unnecessary. The patch modifies the release_vswap_slot() function to take an additional argument for the number of slots to be released, allowing multiple frees to be batched together. This change improves performance by minimizing the number of lock acquisitions and releases.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 22/22] vswap: cache cluster lookup
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a per-CPU cache to store the most recently used vswap cluster during vswap operations, allowing consecutive lookups on the same cluster to skip traversing the global xarray. This improves performance for swap-heavy workloads by reducing the cost of xa_load operations. The cache is cleared when a lookup targets a different cluster, the cached cluster becomes empty, or the CPU goes offline. A reference is held on the cached cluster to keep it alive.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series, based on Linux kernel version 6.19, aims to improve performance and reduce overhead in virtual swap space management. The changes focus on reducing CPU and memory usage on fast swap backends such as zram and PMEM. Key improvements include combining multiple vswap operations, batching per-entry operations, eliminating redundant lookups, and optimizing the reverse map to minimize physical-to-virtual swap indirection costs.

patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Implementation of proactive writeback for zswap memory, raising questions about the necessity and feasibility of this approach.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Questioned the need for proactive writeback, suggesting that existing memory.reclaim mechanism could suffice. Asked if there's a specific reason why zswap memory is treated differently.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Agreed with Nhat's question, expressing skepticism about the need for proactive writeback. Suggested that memory.zswap.max could be used to minimize compressed memory and increase writeback rates.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 12:49 PM Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Mon, May 11, 2026 at 3:52 AM Hao Jia <jiahao.kernel@gmail.com> wrote:
> >
> > From: Hao Jia <jiahao1@lixiang.com>
> >
> > Zswap currently writes back pages to backing swap devices reactively,
> > triggered either by memory pressure via the shrinker or by the pool
> > reaching its size limit. This reactive approach offers no precise
> > control over when writeback happens, which can disturb latency-sensitive
> > workloads, and it cannot direct writeback at a specific memory cgroup.
> > However, there are scenarios where users might want to proactively
> > write back cold pages from zswap to the backing swap device, for
> > example, to free up memory for other applications or to prepare for
> > upcoming memory-intensive workloads.
> >
> > Therefore, implement a proactive writeback mechanism for zswap by
> > adding a new cgroup interface file memory.zswap.proactive_writeback
> > within the memory controller.
>
>
> We already have memory.reclaim, no? Would that not work to create
> headroom generally for your use case? Is there a reason why we are
> treating zswap memory as special here?

+1, why do we need to specifically proactively reclaim the compressed memory?

Also, if we do need to minimize the compressed memory and force higher
writeback rates, we can do so with memory.zswap.max, right?

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v7 00/15] mm/mglru: improve reclaim loop and dirty folio handling
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch series improves the reclaim loop and dirty folio handling in the mm/mglru module, addressing issues related to memory cgroup (memcg) management and fairness. The changes aim to enhance the performance and efficiency of memory allocation and deallocation processes. The approach taken involves refining the existing code to better handle memory pressure and reclaiming, with a focus on improving the overall system responsiveness and stability.

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary:
reviewer mentioned that a systematic test run for one kernel version or config takes about one to two days, and comparing versions/setups takes even longer

Reviewer noted that the patch involves mixed writeback, which is a practical case causing a regression, and requested its inclusion for evaluation.

reviewer noted that the current test is focused on writeback and not related to SWAP, suggesting it's better to avoid noise and irrelevant parts by excluding SWAP from the test

The reviewer provided detailed test results for a fairness evaluation of the mm/mglru patch, including throughput and latency metrics, as well as a fairness index calculated using Jain's fairness metric. The reviewer noted that the current implementation seems fair but requested additional information on why this workload is chosen and its importance for evaluation.

Reviewer Kairui Song noted that MGLRU significantly outperforms classical LRU in a test case provided by the CachyOS community, exhibiting lower jitter and better performance, but did not include this information due to concerns about making the cover letter too long.

Reviewer noted that since the kernel source code is on tmpfs, it's not a realistic representation of typical usage and suggested testing with source code on disk to better understand I/O noise.

The point is not which is better but documenting the performance difference
between them for the given workload.

At the high level, I am just asking for a given benchmark/workload, let's add a
sentence why we think this specific workload is important to measure and
evaluate reclaim mechanism.

patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch restricts the remapping of zero-page mappings to only underused Transparent Huge Pages (THP) splits, addressing potential performance issues when KSM and always-THP policies are enabled together.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that KSM is not suitable for big servers due to its memory management approach, specifically mentioning the use of THPs and 4K chunks, and suggested avoiding setting THP policy to 'always' when enabling KSM

Reviewer pointed out that even if memory is managed in 4K chunks, compaction can still create THPs elsewhere, which contradicts the assumption made by the patch to restrict zero-page remapping to underused THP splits.
review_comments.raw_body:
IIRC, QEMU will, as default, set MADV_HUGEPAGE and MADV_MERGEABLE :)

(KSM itself later has to be enabled manually on a system level)

---

Right. But the 4k chunks are movable and compaction can move them around to
create THPs elsewhere.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that enabling KSM and setting THP policy to always can lead to performance issues, as KSM reduces the occurrence of transparent huge pages; he suggested disabling THPs before enabling KSM for optimal results.
review_comments.raw_body:
Digging around, RHEL documents that one might want to consider disabling THPs
for performance:

"As KSM can reduce the occurence of transparent huge pages, you may want to
disable it before enabling THP." [1]

But that doesn't mean that some people are using that combination.

In the end "some THPs" is better than "no THPs" :)

[1]
https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/red_hat_enterprise_linux/6/html/virtualization_tuning_and_optimization_guide/sect-virtualization_tuning_optimization_guide-memory-huge_pages

Cheers,

David
